title: Reproducing a 1989 Neural Network Paper in 2022
excerpt: Can we improve on a 33-year-old neural network paper using modern deep learning techniques? An exploration of progress in deep learning through reproduction.
content:
Introduction

The Yann LeCun et al. (1989) paper 'Backpropagation Applied to Handwritten Zip Code Recognition' is of historical significance because it is, to my knowledge, the earliest real-world application of a neural net trained end-to-end with backpropagation.

I set out to reproduce this paper to understand the nature of progress in deep learning over the past 33 years.

The Original Architecture

The original network consisted of:

  • 256 input units (16x16 grayscale images)
  • Layer H1: 768 hidden units (12 x 64)
  • Layer H2: 192 hidden units (12 x 16)
  • Layer H3: 30 hidden units
  • 10 output units (one for each digit)

Original Results vs Modern Reproduction

The original paper reported:

eval: split train. loss 2.5e-3. error 0.14%. misses: 10
eval: split test . loss 1.8e-2. error 5.00%. misses: 102

My training script in its current form prints at the end of the 23rd pass:

eval: split train. loss 4.073383e-03. error 0.62%. misses: 45
eval: split test . loss 2.838382e-02. error 4.09%. misses: 82

Key Learnings

Despite 33 years of progress, the improvements are more incremental than revolutionary. The original paper's architecture and approach remain remarkably effective.

What Changed?

  • Better optimization algorithms (Adam vs SGD)
  • Improved initialization techniques
  • Regularization methods (dropout, batch normalization)
  • More efficient GPU utilization

Conclusion

This exercise demonstrates that while we've made significant progress in deep learning, the fundamental principles established in 1989 remain sound. The real progress has been in our ability to scale these techniques to larger datasets and more complex problems.

title: Getting Started with Python: A Beginner's Guide
excerpt: Everything you need to know to start your Python programming journey, from installation to writing your first programs.
content:
Why Python?

Python is one of the most popular programming languages in the world, and for good reason. It's:

  • Easy to learn and read
  • Versatile (web dev, data science, AI, automation)
  • Has a huge community and ecosystem
  • Great for beginners and professionals alike

Installing Python

First, you'll need to install Python on your system:

Windows

  1. Download Python from python.org
  2. Run the installer
  3. Check 'Add Python to PATH'
  4. Click 'Install Now'

Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)

sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3 python3-pip

Your First Python Program

Let's write the classic 'Hello World' program:

print('Hello, World!')

Save this as hello.py and run it:

python hello.py

Variables and Data Types

Python has several built-in data types:

# Numbers
age = 25
pi = 3.14159

# Strings
name = 'Kanchana'
greeting = 'Hello, World!'

# Booleans
is_student = True
is_graduated = False

# Lists
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'orange']

# Dictionaries
person = {
    'name': 'Kanchana',
    'age': 25,
    'job': 'Developer'
}

Control Flow

If Statements

temperature = 25

if temperature > 30:
    print('It\'s hot!')
elif temperature > 20:
    print('It\'s warm!')
else:
    print('It\'s cold!')

Loops

# For loop
for i in range(5):
    print(f'Number: {i}')

# While loop
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(f'Count: {count}')
    count += 1

Functions

def greet(name):
    return f'Hello, {name}!'

def add_numbers(a, b):
    return a + b

# Using functions
message = greet('Kanchana')
print(message)  # Hello, Kanchana!

result = add_numbers(5, 3)
print(result)  # 8

Next Steps

Now that you know the basics, here are some great next steps:

  • Build small projects (calculator, todo list, etc.)
  • Learn about modules and packages
  • Explore web frameworks like Flask or Django
  • Dive into data science with pandas and NumPy
  • Try automation with scripts
The best way to learn programming is by doing. Start building things, make mistakes, and learn from them!

title: Building a Windows 95 Themed Portfolio
excerpt: How I created a fully functional Windows 95 interface using only HTML, CSS, and vanilla JavaScript.
content:
The Inspiration

I wanted my portfolio to stand out. Instead of going with the typical modern design, I decided to recreate the nostalgia of Windows 95.

Core Features

  • Draggable windows with minimize/maximize/close buttons
  • Working taskbar with clock
  • Start menu with program launcher
  • MS-DOS prompt with actual commands
  • Desktop icons with selection
  • Windows Media Player with playlist

Technical Implementation

Window System

The window system uses vanilla JavaScript:

function startDrag(event, windowId) {
    draggedWindow = document.getElementById(windowId);
    setActiveWindow(windowId);
    
    const rect = draggedWindow.getBoundingClientRect();
    dragOffsetX = event.clientX - rect.left;
    dragOffsetY = event.clientY - rect.top;
}

Styling

Recreating the Windows 95 aesthetic required attention to detail:

.window {
    background: #c0c0c0;
    border: 2px solid;
    border-color: #dfdfdf #000000 #000000 #dfdfdf;
    box-shadow: 2px 2px 0 rgba(0,0,0,0.2);
}

Key Learnings

Building this portfolio taught me that constraints breed creativity. Working within the Windows 95 design system forced me to think differently about UI/UX.

This project proves you don't need the latest frameworks to build something impressive!

title: The Complete Guide to .gitignore — Everything You Need to Know
excerpt: Learn everything about .gitignore from scratch. What it is, how it works, syntax and patterns, real examples for Node.js, Python, Java, and more. A must-read for every developer.
content:
What Is .gitignore?

If you use Git to manage your code, you have probably heard of the .gitignore file. But what exactly is it, and why does every developer need it?

A .gitignore file is a plain text file that tells Git which files and folders to ignore. When you run git add or git commit, Git looks at this file and skips everything listed in it. Those files will not be tracked, staged, or committed.

In simple terms: .gitignore keeps your Git repository clean by excluding files that do not belong there — like passwords, build folders, logs, and editor config files.

Without .gitignore, you might accidentally commit API keys, huge build folders, or OS junk files. These cause security risks, slow down your repo, and clutter your project history.

How Does .gitignore Work?

When Git checks your project folder for changes, it reads the .gitignore file. Any file or folder that matches a pattern inside it is completely skipped. It is as if those files do not exist from Git's perspective.

Here is the basic flow:

  1. You edit files in your project
  2. You run git add .
  3. Git checks each file against the .gitignore rules
  4. Matched files are skipped — unmatched files are staged

This check happens every time you run Git commands. Rules are read from top to bottom, and later rules can override earlier ones.

Where Do You Put the .gitignore File?

You can place .gitignore files in several locations. Each one affects a different scope:

  • Root .gitignore — The most common. Place it in your project's main folder. It covers the whole project and should be committed so your team shares the same rules.
  • Subfolder .gitignore — Rules only apply to that folder and its contents.
  • Global .gitignore — Applies to every Git project on your computer. Great for editor files like .DS_Store or Thumbs.db.
  • .git/info/exclude — Local rules that are never shared with others. Only you see them.

How to Create a .gitignore File

Creating a .gitignore file is very simple. Here are the main ways:

Method 1 — Create It Manually

Open your terminal in your project folder and run:

touch .gitignore

Then open the file in any text editor and add your rules.

Method 2 — Use GitHub When Creating a Repo

When you create a new repository on GitHub, it offers a dropdown to pick a .gitignore template based on your language (Node, Python, Java, etc.). This is the fastest way to get started.

Method 3 — Use gitignore.io

Visit toptal.com/developers/gitignore. Type your tools (like "Node", "Python", "macOS", "VSCode") and it generates a ready-to-use file. Just copy and paste.

Method 4 — Set a Global gitignore

git config --global core.excludesfile ~/.gitignore_global

.gitignore Syntax and Patterns

The rules inside a .gitignore file are called patterns. Here is a full breakdown:

Basic Rules

  • # comment — Lines starting with # are comments. Git ignores them.
  • filename.txt — Ignores a specific file anywhere in the project.
  • foldername/ — Ignores an entire folder.
  • *.ext — Ignores all files with a given extension.
  • !filename — Un-ignores a previously ignored file (negation).
  • /filename — Ignores only in the root folder.
  • folder/** — Ignores everything inside a folder recursively.
  • **/*.ext — Ignores files with that extension in any subfolder.
  • file?.txt — The ? matches exactly one character.
  • [abc].txt — Matches one of the characters listed in brackets.

Understanding Wildcards

  • * (asterisk) — Matches anything except a slash. Use for file names.
  • ** (double asterisk) — Matches anything including slashes. Use for deep paths.
  • ? (question mark) — Matches exactly one character.
  • [ ] (brackets) — Matches any one character listed inside.

Negation — The ! Trick

You can ignore a folder but keep specific files inside it using the ! prefix:

# Ignore all log files
*.log

# But keep this important one
!important.log

# Ignore the build folder
build/

# But keep one specific file inside it
!build/README.md

Important limitation: You cannot un-ignore a file that is inside an ignored directory. If you ignore build/, then !build/file.txt will NOT work. To fix this, ignore specific files inside the folder instead of the whole folder.

Order of Rules

Rules are read from top to bottom. Later rules win over earlier ones. Also, a .gitignore in a subfolder overrides the root one for that subfolder. The rule closest to your file always has the highest priority.

Full .gitignore Examples by Language

Node.js / JavaScript

# Dependencies
node_modules/

# Build output
dist/
build/

# Logs
*.log
npm-debug.log*
yarn-error.log*

# Environment variables (NEVER commit these)
.env
.env.local
.env.*.local

# OS files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Editor
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp

Python

# Byte-compiled files
__pycache__/
*.py[cod]
*.pyo

# Virtual environments
venv/
env/
.env/

# Distribution / packaging
dist/
build/
*.egg-info/

# Testing
.pytest_cache/
.coverage
htmlcov/

# Jupyter Notebook
.ipynb_checkpoints/

# Environment variables
.env
*.env

Java / Maven / Gradle

# Compiled class files
*.class

# Package files
*.jar
*.war
*.ear

# Build directories
target/
build/
.gradle/

# IDE files
.idea/
*.iml
.eclipse/

# Logs
*.log

React / Next.js

# Dependencies
node_modules/

# Next.js build output
.next/
out/

# Production
build/

# Environment files
.env*
!.env.example

# Vercel
.vercel

General / OS Files

# macOS
.DS_Store
.AppleDouble

# Windows
Thumbs.db
Desktop.ini

# Linux
*~

# Editor backups
*.bak
*.tmp
*.swp

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/

Important Concepts You Must Know

Already Tracked Files Are NOT Ignored

This is the most common mistake beginners make. If you already committed a file to Git, adding it to .gitignore will not make Git forget it. You need to untrack it first:

# Step 1: Add the file to .gitignore
# Step 2: Stop tracking it
git rm --cached filename.txt

# For a folder
git rm -r --cached foldername/

# Step 3: Commit the change
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Stop tracking filename.txt"

The file stays on your computer. Git just stops tracking it going forward.

The .gitignore File Itself Should Be Committed

Yes! The .gitignore file is meant to be committed and shared with your team. Everyone on the project should use the same ignore rules. Always run:

git add .gitignore
git commit -m "Add gitignore"
git push

Checking If a File Is Being Ignored

You can check if a specific file is being ignored — and which rule is causing it:

git check-ignore -v filename.txt

To see all currently ignored files in your project:

git status --ignored

Setting Up a Global .gitignore

A global .gitignore applies to all Git projects on your computer. This is great for editor and OS files you never want to commit anywhere.

# Step 1: Create the file
touch ~/.gitignore_global

# Step 2: Tell Git about it
git config --global core.excludesfile ~/.gitignore_global

Then add your rules to ~/.gitignore_global:

# macOS
.DS_Store

# Windows
Thumbs.db

# VSCode
.vscode/

# JetBrains IDEs
.idea/

# Vim
*.swp
*.swo

Security — What to ALWAYS Ignore

One of the most important uses of .gitignore is keeping sensitive data out of your repository. Once a secret is pushed to GitHub (even to a private repo), it should be considered compromised.

Files you should always ignore:

  • .env — environment variables (API keys, passwords, database URLs)
  • *.pem, *.key, *.cert — SSL/TLS certificates and private keys
  • id_rsa, id_ed25519 — SSH private keys
  • credentials.json — Google service account keys
  • serviceAccountKey.json — Firebase admin keys
  • *.keystore — Android signing keys
If you ever accidentally commit a secret, change it immediately — rotate the key or password. Then use git filter-branch or BFG Repo Cleaner to remove it from history.

Common .gitignore Mistakes and How to Fix Them

  • Ignoring a file that was already committed — Use git rm --cached to untrack it first.
  • Trying to un-ignore files inside an ignored folder — Ignore specific files, not the whole folder.
  • Using backslashes on Windows — Always use forward slashes / in .gitignore.
  • Forgetting to commit the .gitignore file itself — Run git add .gitignore and commit it.
  • Only ignoring .env but forgetting .env.local — Use .env* to catch all variants.
  • Copying someone else's .gitignore without reviewing it — Always read and customize for your project.

Quick Reference Cheat Sheet

# Exact file name
filename.txt

# All files with this extension
*.txt

# Files only in docs/ folder
docs/*.txt

# Files in docs/ and all subfolders
docs/**/*.txt

# Entire folder
build/

# Only in root folder
/config.js

# Character wildcard
temp-?

# Character set
[Tt]emp

# Negation (un-ignore)
*.log
!important.log

# Comment
# This line is ignored by Git

Frequently Asked Questions

Does .gitignore hide files from my computer?

No. .gitignore only affects Git. Your files still exist on your computer. Git just does not track or commit them.

Can I have more than one .gitignore file?

Yes! You can have as many as you need — one per folder. They all work together. The one closest to your file takes priority.

Why is my file still being tracked after I added it to .gitignore?

Because the file was already committed before you added the rule. Run git rm --cached filename to stop tracking it, then commit that change.

Should I commit .gitignore?

Yes, always. It is part of your project and your whole team needs it.

Is .gitignore case-sensitive?

It depends on your OS. On Linux it is case-sensitive. On macOS and Windows it may not be. Write patterns in the exact case of your file names to be safe.

Summary

Here is everything we covered:

  • .gitignore is a text file that tells Git which files and folders to skip.
  • It keeps your repository clean, fast, and secure.
  • Patterns use wildcards like *, **, and ? to match multiple files.
  • Rules are read top-to-bottom. Later rules win.
  • Already-committed files must be untracked with git rm --cached.
  • Always ignore .env and other secret files for security.
  • Commit your .gitignore so your whole team benefits.
  • Use gitignore.io to generate templates for your stack.
Pro tip: Start every new project by creating a .gitignore before your first commit. It is much easier to ignore files from the start than to remove them from Git history later.
